title: Pet-safe fabric deodoriser spray
subtitle: A vinegar and water spray for deodorising pet bedding and upholstery without essential oils
excerpt: White vinegar neutralises pet odours rather than masking them: when it dries, the acetic acid smell dissipates and takes the odour molecules with it. This spray contains no essential oils, making it safe to use in homes with cats, dogs, rabbits, and birds. It is suitable for fabric sofas, pet bedding, rugs, and upholstery. Yield is approximately 250 ml.
sourceNotes: White vinegar as a fabric deodoriser: the acetic acid in white vinegar reacts with alkaline odour compounds (such as ammonia in urine) and neutralises them. When the vinegar dries, the acetic acid itself evaporates, taking the odour with it. The smell of vinegar disappears as it dries; there is no residual vinegar scent once the fabric is fully dry. Vegetable glycerine at 2%: a small amount of humectant helps the spray spread on fabric fibres without leaving a residue. No essential oils: all common essential oils are toxic to cats, and many are harmful to birds; the formulation deliberately avoids them. Test on an inconspicuous area before use on delicate fabrics.

recipe.yieldDescription: Approximately 250 ml in a spray bottle
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the recipe for a larger bottle.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use immediately. Keeps well at room temperature.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months at room temperature. White vinegar solutions are self-preserving. Discard if the spray develops an unusual appearance.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between an odour-causing compound and a neutralising agent that eliminates the smell rather than masking it with fragrance. White vinegar neutralises alkaline odour compounds such as ammonia; when the vinegar dries, the acetic acid also evaporates, leaving no residual scent.

content.text: Most pet odour sprays mask the smell with fragrance. This one uses

: white vinegar reacts with the compounds in pet urine and animal odour and breaks them down. The vinegar smell fades as fabric dries; it does not linger. The spray has no essential oils, making it safe for homes with cats, dogs, rabbits, guinea pigs, and birds. Essential oils can be toxic to cats and birds; leaving them out makes this spray safe for multi-pet homes. Test on a hidden area before spraying delicate or light-coloured fabrics.

items.prepNote: 5% acetic acid; the active neutralising agent
Helps the spray spread on fabric fibres; does not leave a sticky residue at this concentration

content.text: Combine all ingredients in the spray bottle or in a jug and stir briefly. Pour into the spray bottle if mixing in a jug. Label with the batch date.

Spray lightly over pet bedding, upholstered furniture, rugs, or car seats that have absorbed pet odour. Allow to dry fully; the vinegar smell will dissipate as the fabric dries. For stubborn odours, spray and blot with a clean cloth, then allow to air-dry. Do not saturate delicate fabrics. Test on an inconspicuous area first on light-coloured or delicate fabrics.
Store at room temperature. The acidity of white vinegar preserves the spray; it keeps 12 months. Discard if the spray develops an unusual appearance or a smell beyond the normal vinegar character.
